LinearProgramming.goal of an optimization problem?: In an optimization problem one seeks to maximize or minimize a specific quantity
LinearProgramming.the objective of an optimization proble: The objective is the quantity that one seeks to maximize or minimize in an optimization problem
LinearProgramming.variables does the objective in an opti: The objective in an optimization problem always depends on finitely many variables.
LinearProgramming.the variables that the objective in an o: If variables are not independent of one another, then their relationships are called constraints
LinearProgramming.a mathematical program?: A mathematical program is an optimization problem in which the objective and constraints are given as mathematical functions and functional relationships,
LinearProgramming.form of a mathematical program?: Mathematical programs have the form:      optimize> z = f(x_1, ..., x_n)     Subject to:g_1(x_1, ..., x_n)     , ...,      g_m(x_1, ..., x_n)     , where each constraint involves one of three signs, \le, =, or \ge.
LinearProgramming.an unconstrained mathematical program?: Unconstrained mathematical programs are covered by the formalism if each function g_i is chosen as zero and each constant b_i is chosen as zero.
LinearProgramming.mathematical program called linear?: A mathematical program is linear if the objective function and each of the constraints are liear in each of their arguments.
LinearProgramming.mathematical program called nonlinear?: Any mathematical program that is not linear is called nonlinear.
LinearProgramming.an integer program?: An integer program is a linear program with the additional restriction that the input variables be integers.
LinearProgramming.a quadratic program?: A quadratic program is a mathematical program in which each of the constraints are linear but the objective function has the form     f(x_1, ..., x_n) = \Sigma_{i = 1 to n}\Sigma_{j = 1to n} c_{ij}x_ix_j + \Sigma_{i = 1 to n}d_ix_i.     Where c_{ij} and d_i are known constants.
LinearProgramming.approach to transform a word problem in: The following approach is recommended to transform a word problem into a mathematical program:      STEP 1: Determine the quantity to be optimized and express it as a mathematical function. Doing so determines the input variables.      STEP 2:Identify all stipulated requirements, restrictions, and limitations, and express them mathematically. These requirements constitute the constraints.      STEP 3:Express any hidden conditions. Such conditions are ont stipulated explicitly in the problem but are apparent from the physical situation being modeled.
LinearProgramming.If multiple solutions exist to a mathema: In a mathematical program we seek a solution. If a number of equally optimal solutions exist, then any one will do.
LinearProgramming.the feasible region?: The feasible region is the set of points, x, satisfying all the constraints, including the nonnegativity conditions.
LinearProgramming.a linear constraint?: Linear constraints are of the form:      \Sigma_{j = 1 to n} a_{ij}x_j ~ b_i, where ~ stands for one of the relations \le, \ge, or =. The constants b_i may always be assumed to be nonnegative.
LinearProgramming.a slack variable?: A linear constraint of the form \Sigmaa_{ij}x_j \le b_i can be converted into an equality by adding a neq nonnegative variable to the left-hand side of the inequality. Such a variable is numerically equal to the difference betwen the right- and left- hand sides of hte inequality and is known as a slack variable.
LinearProgramming.a surplus variable?: A linear constraint of the form \Sigmaa_{ij}x_j \ge b_i can be converted into an equality by subtracting a new, nonnegative variable from the left-hand side of the inequality. Such a variable is numerically equal to the difference betwen the right- and left- hand sides of hte inequality and is known as a surplus variable.
LinearProgramming.an artificial variable?: After all linear constraints, with nonnegative right hand sides, have been transformed into equalities by intriducing slack and surplus variables where necessary, add a new variable, called an artificial variable to the left hand side of each constraint that does not contain a slack variable.
LinearProgramming.nonnegative initial solution to a set of: A nonnegative initial solution to the set of equality constraints where each constraint has either a slack or an artificial variable can be found by setting each slack variable and each artificial variable equal to the right hand side of the equation in which it appears and setting all other variables (including the surplus variables) equal to zero.
LinearProgramming.coefficient of slack variables in the o: Since the introduction of slack variables does not change the problem, their coefficients in the objective function are always 0.
LinearProgramming.coefficient of surplus variables in the: Since the introduction of surplus variables does not change the problem, their coefficients in the objective function are always 0.
LinearProgramming.artificial variables change the linear: Artifiicial variables can change the nature of constraints since they add to only one side of an equality.
LinearProgramming.coefficient of artificial variables in: In order to ensure that artificial variables do not change the nature of the problem, they are given a large negative coefficients in a maximization problem, or large positive coefficients in a minimization problem.
LinearProgramming.notation for very large positive or neg: The notation for very large positive or negative coefficients are M and -M respectively, where M is understood to be a large positive number.
LinearProgramming.the standard form of a linear program?: A linear program is in standard form if the constraints are all modeled as equalities and id one feasible solution is known. In matrix dorm, standard form is:      Optimize: z = C^TX     subject to:AX = B     with X \ge 0, where X is the column vector of unknowns, including all slack, surplus, and artificial variables;C^T is the row vector of the corresponding costs;A is the coefficient matrix of the constraint equations;and B is the column vector of the right hand sides of the constraint equations.
LinearProgramming.At the start of a linear program, the v: At the start of a linear program, X_0 (the basis) is the vector of slack and artificial variables only.
LinearProgramming.initial value of the X_0 vector if we h: The initial feasible solution is given by X_0 = B, where it is understood that all variables in X not included in X_0 are assigned 0 values.
LinearProgramming.a set of vectors being linearly indepen: A set of m-dimensional vectors {P_1, ..., P_n} is linearly dependent if there exist constants \alpha_1, ..., \alpha_n, not all zero such that      \alpha_1P_1 + ... + \alpha_nP_n = 0
LinearProgramming.a set of vectors being linearly depende: A set of m-dimensional vectors {P_1, ..., P_n} is linearly independent if the only constants for which      \alpha_1P_1 + ... + \alpha_nP_n = 0     holds are \alpha_1 = ... = \alpha_n = 0
LinearProgramming.a vector being a convex combination of: An m-dimensional vector P is a convex combination of the m-dimensional vectors P_1, ..., P_n if there exist nonnegative constants \beta_1, ..., \beta_n whose sum is 1, such that      P = \beta_1P_1 + ... + \beta_nP_n
LinearProgramming.a line segment between two vectors?: Given two m-dimensional vectors, P_1 and P_2, we call the set of all convex combinations of P_1 and P_2 the line segment between the two vectors.
LinearProgramming.a convex set of vectors?: A set of m-dimensional vectors is convex if whenever two vectors belong to the set, then so too does the line segment between the two vectors.
LinearProgramming.an extreme point of a convex set?: A vector P is an extreme point of a convex set if it cannot be expressed as a convex combination of two other vectors in the set;that is, an extreme point does not lie on the line segment between any other two vectors in the set.
LinearProgramming.vectors in compact convex sets related t: Any vector in a closed and Bounded convex set with a finite number of extreme points can be expressed as a convex combination of the extreme points.
LinearProgramming.the solution space of simultaneous linea: The solution space of simultaneous linear equations is a convex set having a finite number of extreme points
LinearProgramming.intersection and convex sets?: If \mathcal{Q} and \mathcal{R} are convex sets then \mathcal{Q} \cap \mathcal{R} is a convex set.
LinearProgramming.extreme points does a linear program in: Let \mathcal{L} designate the set of all feasible solutions to the linear program in standard form, that is \mathcal{L} is the set of all vectors X that satisfy AX = B and X \ge 0. \mathcal{L} has finitely many extreme points
LinearProgramming.objective function always attains its ma: The objective function attains its maximum or minimum at an extreme point of \mathcal{L}, provided an optimum exists.
LinearProgramming.If A is m \\times n, with m \\ge n, ext: If A has order m \times n (m rows nad n columns), with m \le n, then extreme points of \mathcal{L} have at least n - m zero components.
A basic feasible solution to x_1A_1 + ... + x_nA_n = B is obtained by setting n - m of the x-variables equal to zero and finding a nonnegative solution for the remaining x-variables, provided the m A-vectors corresponding to the x-variables not set equal to zero are linearly independent.
LinearProgramming.the basic variables of our solution?: The x-variables not initially zet equal to zero are called basic variables.
LinearProgramming.the basic feasible solution to be degene: If one or more basic variables turns out to be zero, the basic feasible solution is degenerate.
LinearProgramming.the basic feasible solution to be nondeg: If all the basic variables are positive, the basic feasible solution is nondegenerate.
LinearProgramming.basic feasible solutions and optimal so: The objective function attains its optimum at a basic feasible solution.
LinearProgramming.basic feasible solutions and extreme po: The extreme points of \mathcal{L} are precisely the basic feasible solutions.
LinearProgramming.standard linear program be solved using: The standard linear program can be solved by seeking among the basic feasible solutions the ones at which the objective is optimized.
LinearProgramming.Q being a linear combination of the vec: A vector Q is a linear combination of the vectors Q_1, ..., Q_n if there exist constants \delta_1, ..., \delta_n such that      Q = \delta_1Q_1 + ... + \delta_nQ_n
LinearProgramming.realationship between being linearly de: The vectors P_1, ..., P_n is linearly dependent if and only if one of the vectors is a linear combination of the rest.
LinearProgramming.being linearly independent and linear c: If {P_1, ..., P_r} is a linearly independent set of vectors and P is a vector such that P = \Sigma_{j = 1 to r}c_jP_j and P = \Sigma_{j = 1 to r}d_jP_j     then c_j = d_j for j = 1, ..., r.
LinearProgramming.linearly independent sets and subsets o: Every nonempty subset of a linearly independent set of vectors is itself linearly independent.
LinearProgramming.relationshp between convex sets and the: If \mathcal{L} and \mathcal{R} are convex sets, then their intersection, \mathcal{L} \cap \mathcal{R} is a convex set.
LinearProgramming.form of linear programs that the simple: The simplex method is a matrix procedure for solving linear programs in the stanard form      optimizeL z = C^TXsubject to:     AX = B     with X \ge 0, where B \ge 0 and a basic feasible solution X_0 is known.
LinearProgramming.vector C_0 represent?: C_0 represents the constraints for the variables in the basic feasible solution.
LinearProgramming.vector X_0 represent?: X_0 represents the variables in the basic feasible solution.
LinearProgramming.initial tableau for the simplex method?:
The initial tableau for the simplex method is
X^TC^T
X_0C_0AB
C^T - C_0^TA-C_0^TB
LinearProgramming.artificial variables change a simplex t: If there are artificial variables in the linear program, then the last line becomes two lines, where the first line is the real numbers, and the second line is the coefficients of the M variables.
LinearProgramming.algorithm for the simplex method?: The Algorithm for the Simplex method is as follows:      1. Locate the most negative number in the bottom row of the simplex tableau, excluding the last column.      2. Designate that column the work column.      3. Form ratio's by dividing each <u>positive</u> number in the work column (excluding the last row) into the element in the same row and last column.      4. Designate the element in the work column with the smallest ratio as the pivot element.      5. If no element in the work column is positive, then the program has no solution.      6. Use elementary row operations to convert the pivot element to 1 and then reduce all other elements in the work column to 0.      7. Replace the x-variable in the pivot row and first column by the x-variable in the first row and pivot column.      8. The new first column is the current set of basic variables.      Repeat steps 1 through 7 until there are no negative numbers in the last row, excluding the last column.      9. Apply steps 1 through 8 to the next-to-last row and elements that are positioned over zero's in the last row.      10. The optimal solution is obtained by assigning to each variable in the first column that value in the corresponding row and last column.      11. All other variables are assigned the value zero.      12. The associated optimal value of hte objective function is the number in the last row and last column (for a minimization problem its the negative of this number).
LinearProgramming.algorithm for the dual simplex method?: The Algorithm for the Dual Siplex method is as follows:      1. Rewrite the linear program by expressing all constraints in \le form.      2. Transform all constraints into equations using slack variables.      3. Exhibit the problem in the form of a simplex tableau.      4. If the optimality condition is satisfied <u>and</u> one or more basic variables have negative values, the dual simplex method is applicable.      5. Feasibility Condition: The basic variable with the most negative value becomes the departing variable (D.V.).      6. Call the row in which the departing variable occurs the work row.      7. Optimality Condition: Form ratios by dividing elements (except the last element) of the last row by the corresponding <u>negative</u> elements of the work row.      8. The nonbasic variable with the smallest absolute ratio becomes the entering variable.      9. Designate this element in the work row as the pivot element and the corresponding column the work column.      10. If no element in the work row is negative, the problem has no feasible solution.      11. Use elementary row operations to vonvert the pivot element to 1 and then reduce all other elements in the work column to zero.      12. Repeat steps 5 through 11 until there are no negative values for the basic variables.
LinearProgramming.Given a linear program that says to mini: Given a linear program:     minimize: z = C^TX     subject to: AX \ge B     with X \ge 0          The linear program      maximize: z = B^TW     subject to: A^TW \le C     with: W\ge 0           is the dual of the original program.
LinearProgramming.Given a linear program that says to maxi: Given a linear program:     maximize: z = C^TX     subject to: AX \le B     with X \ge 0          The linear program      minimize: z = B^TW     subject to: A^TW \ge C     with: W\ge 0           is the dual of the original program.
LinearProgramming.symmetric duals?: Given a primal in nonstandard form and its dual, they are called symmetric duals of one another.
LinearProgramming.shadow costs?: I don't understand what shadow costs are.
LinearProgramming.duality theorem?: (Duality Theorem): If an optimal solution exists to either the primal or symmetric dual program, then the other program also has an optimal solution and the two objective functions have the same optimal value.
LinearProgramming.relationshp between the optimal solutio: (Duality Theorem): If an optimal solution exists to either the primal or symmetric dual program, then the other program also has an optimal solution and the two objective functions have the same optimal value.
LinearProgramming.Where do we read the optimal solution of: The optimal solution to the primal is found in the last row of the final simplex tableau for the dual, in those columns associated with the slack or surplus variables.
LinearProgramming.Where do we read the optimal solution to: The optimal solution to the dual is found in the last row of the final simplex tableau for the primal, in those columns associated with the slack or surplus variables.
LinearProgramming.dual variables and slack/surplus variab: Given that the pair of symmetric duals have optimal solutions, then if the kth constant of one system holds as an inequality (which means the associated slack or surplus variable is positive), then the kth component of the optimal solution of its symmetric dual is zero.
LinearProgramming.the gradient of a constraint?: If a linear program is given in the form min {c^Tx | a_i^Tx \le b_i, for i = 1, ..., m}, then the vector a_i is called the gradient of the ith constraint.
LinearProgramming.an inactive constraint of a linear prog: Constraint i is called inactive at x_0 if a_ix_0 <b_i
LinearProgramming.an active constraint of a linear progra: Constraint i is called active at x_0 if a_ix_0 = b_i
LinearProgramming.a violated constraint of a linear progr: Constraint i is called violated at x_0 if a_ix_0 >b_i
LinearProgramming.an optimal solution for a minimization: \bar{x} is called an optimal solution if \bar{x} is feasible and c^T\bar{x} \le c^Tx for all feasible x.
LinearProgramming.an optimal solution for a maximization: \bar{x} is called an optimal solution if \bar{x} is feasible and c^T\bar{x} \ge c^Tx for all feasible x.
LinearProgramming.a polyhedron?: A polyhedron is a set that cab be expressed in the form {x \in \mathbb{R} | Ax \ge b}, wheere A is an m \times n matrix and b is a vector in \mathbb{R}^m
LinearProgramming.a bounded set?: A set S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n is bounded if there is a constant k s.t. the absolute vale of every component of every element of S is less than or equal to k.
LinearProgramming.a hyperplane?: The set {x \in \mathbb{R}^n | z'x = b} is called a hyperplane.
LinearProgramming.a halfspace?: The set {x \in \mathbb{R}^n | z'x \ge b} is called a halfspace.
LinearProgramming.a convex set?: A set S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n is convex if for any x, y \in S and any \lambda \in [0, 1] we have \lambdax + (1 - \lambda)y \in S
LinearProgramming.a convex combination of vectors?: Let x^1, ..., x^k be vectors in \mathbb{R}^n and let \lambda_1, ..., \lambda_k be nonnegative scalars whose sum = 1.      .The vector \sigma_{i = 1 to k} \lambda_1x^1 is said to be a convex combination of the vectors x^1, ..., x^k
LinearProgramming.the convex hull of a set of vectors?: The convex hull of the vectors x^1, ..., x^k is the set of all such convex combinations.
LinearProgramming.the union/intersection of convex sets a: The intersection of convex sets is convex
LinearProgramming.polyhedrons and convex sets? why?: Every polyhedron is a convex set.
LinearProgramming.convex sets and convex combinations of: A convex combination of a finite number of elements of a convex set also belongs to that set.
LinearProgramming.convex hulls and convexity?: The convex hull of a finite number of vectors is a convex set.
LinearProgramming.an extreme point of a polyhedron?: Let P be a polyhedron. A vector x \in P is an extreme point of P if we cannot find two vectors y, z \in P, both different from x, and a scalar \lambda \in [0, 1] s.t. x = \lambday + (1 - \lambda)z.
LinearProgramming.a vertex of a polyhedron?: Let P be a polyhedron. A vector x \in P is a vertex of P if there is some c s.t. c'x //lt c'y for all y satisfying y \in P and y \ne x.      (So x is a verex of P \Rightarrow P is on one side of a hyperplane which meets P only at x.)
LinearProgramming.an active or binding constraint?: If a vertx x* satisfies a'x = b for some i in M, ..., or M we say that the corresponding constraint is active or binding at x*.
LinearProgramming.linear independence and systems of equa: Let x* be an element of \mathbb{R}^n and let I { i | ai'x = bi } be the set of indices of constraints that are active at x*. Then the following are equivalent:      - There exist n vectors in the set {ai | i \in I }, which are linearly independent.      - The span of the vectors ai, i \in I is all of \mathbb{R}-sup-n, that is, every element of \mathbb{R}-sup-n can be expressed as a linear combination of the vectors ai, i \in I.      - The system of equations ai'x = bi, i \in I has a unique solution.
LinearProgramming.a basic solution?: The vector x* is a basic solution if      (i) all equality constraints are active.      (ii) Out of the constraints hat are active at x* there are n of them that are linearly independent.
LinearProgramming.a basic feasible solution?: If x* is a basic solution that satisfies all of the constraints, then we say that x is a basic feasible dimension.
LinearProgramming.minimum number of constraints required: If the number, m, of constraints used to define a polyhedron P [subst] \mathbb{R}-sup-n is less than n, the number of active onstraints at any given point must also be less than n and here are no basic feasible solutions.
LinearProgramming.vertices, extreme points, and basic sol: THE FOLLOWING ARE EQUIVALENT:     - x* is a vertex.      - x* is an extreme point.      - x* is a basic feasible solution.
LinearProgramming.basic feasible sollutions can a finite: Given a finite number of linear inequality constraints, there can onl be a finite number of basic feasible solutions.
LinearProgramming.adjacent basic feasible solutions?: Two distinct basic feasible solutions to a set of linear constraints n \mathbb{R}-sup-n are said to be adjacent if we can find a-1 linearly indepenedet constraints that are active at both of them.
LinearProgramming.adjacent basic feasible solutions imply: If two adjacent basic feasible solutions are also feasble, the the line segment the joins them is called an edge of the feasible set.
LinearProgramming.a feasible direction?: Let x be an element of a polyhedron P. A vector d \in \mathbb{R}^n is said to be a feasible direction at x if there exists a positive scalar \theta for which x + \thetad \in P.
LinearProgramming.jth basic direction and how is it compu: If d is the direction vector, where d_j = 1 and d_i = 0 for every other nonbasic i, then d_B = B^{-1}d_j when we have a minimum problem. This is called the jth basic direction.
LinearProgramming.a nondegenerate BFS and directions?: For a nonbasic BFS, any basic direction is also a basic feasible direction.
LinearProgramming.-c_B^T]B[sup -1]A[sub j] measure?: -c_B^T]B[sup -1]A[sub j] is the cost of compensating change in the basic variables necessitated by the consaraint Ax = b.
LinearProgramming.reduced cost?: Let x be a BS, let B be an associated basis matrix, and let c_B be the vector of costs of the basic variables. For each j, we define the reduced cost //bar{c}_j of the variable x_j according to the formula //bar{c}_j = c_j - c_B^TB[sup -1]A[sub j]
LinearProgramming.What quantities does the reduced cost ve: The reduced cost vector depends upon the coefficient of the associated variable in the original objective function, the objective coefficients for the basic variables, the current basis matrix, and the column vector associated with the associated variable.
LinearProgramming.reduced cost measure?: Reduced cost measures cost of increase vs cost of change
LinearProgramming.reduced cost of a basic variable: For every basic variable, we have -c_B^T]B[sup -1]A[sub B(i)] = 0, so the reduced cost of a basic variable is zero.
LinearProgramming.optimality of a nondegerate solution and: Consider a BFS x associated with basis matrix B and let //bar{c} be the corresponding vector of reduced costs.      (a) If //bar{c} \ge 0, then x is optimal.      (b) If x is optimal and nondegenerate, then //bar{c} \ge 0.
LinearProgramming.we know if a nondegerate BFS is optimal: In order to decide if a nondegerate BFS is optimal, we need only check whether all reduced costs are nonnegative.
LinearProgramming.we know if a basis matrix B is optimal?: A basis matrix B is said to be optimal if      (a) B[sup -1]b \ge 0 and      (b) //bar{c}^T] = c^T] - c_B^T]B[sup -1]A \ge 0
LinearProgramming.What can we imply about the reduced cost: If the reduced cost //bar{c}_j of a nonbasic variable x_j is negative, the jth basic direction is a feasible direction of cost decrease. This takes us to the point x + \theta*d, where \theta* = max {\theta \ge 0 | x + \thetad \in P}. The resulting cost change is \theta*c^Td.
LinearProgramming.maximum length we can traval along a fe: If d_i //lt 0 for some i, then the largest possible value of \theta is      \theta* = min_{{i = 1, ..., m | d_{B(i)} //lt 0}}(-x_{B(i)} / d_{B(i)})
LinearProgramming.conversions between primal variables and: Let A be a matrix with rows ai and columns Aj. Given a primal problem with the structure shown on the left, its ual is defined to be the maximization problem shown on the right.      Primal     min c'x     s.t.      . a_i'x \ge b_i i \in M_1     . a_i'x \le b_i i \in M_2     . a_i'x = b_i i \in M_3     x_jj \ge 0 j \in N_11     x_jj \le 0 j \in N_2     x_jj free j \in N_3          Dual     max p'b     s.t.      p_i \ge 0 i \in M_11     p_i \le 0 i \in M_2     p_i free i \in M_3     . p'a_j \le c_j i \in M_1     . p'a_j \ge c_j i \in M_2     . p'a_i = c_j i \in M_3
LinearProgramming.constraints about the primal imply about: For each constraint in the primal we introduce a variable in the dual problem
LinearProgramming.variables about the primal imply about t: For each variable in the primal problem we introduce a constraint in the dual problem.
LinearProgramming.dual of the dual of a primal problem?: If we transform the dual into an equivalent minimization problem and then form its dual we obtain a problem equivalent to the original problem.
LinearProgramming.the duals of equivalent primal problems: Suppose that we have transformed a linear programming problem //Pi1 to another linear programming problem //Pi2 by a sequence of transformations of the following types:      - replace free variables with the difference of two nonnegative vaiables.      - replace an inequality constraint by an equalit constraint involving nonnegative slack variables     - If some row of the matrix A in a feasible standard form problem is a linear combination of the other two rows, eliminate the correspondng constraint.      Then the duals of //Pi1 and //Pi2 are equivalent, i.e. they are both infeasible, or they have the same optimal cost.
LinearProgramming.Weak Duality Theorem?: (Weak Duality Theorem) if x is a feasible solution to the primal problem and p is a feasible solution to the dual problem, p'b \le c'x (in other words, the max problem is always less than the min problem).
LinearProgramming.an unbounded primal imply about the dual: If the optimal cost for the primal is -//infty then the dual prblem is infeasible.
LinearProgramming.an unbounded dual imply about the primal: If the optimal cost of the dual is //infty, then the primal must be infeasible.
LinearProgramming.What can we imply from feasible solution: Let x and p be feasible solutions to he primal and the dual respectively adn suppose p'b = c'x. Then x and p ae optimal solutions to the primal and dual respectively.
LinearProgramming.Strong Duality Theorem?: (Strong Duality Theorem) If a linear programming problem has an optimal solution, so does its dual, and the respective optimal costs are equal.
LinearProgramming.Complementary Slacness Theorem?: (Complementary Slackness) Let x and p be feasible solutions to the primal and the dual problem respectively, The vectors x and p are optimal solutions for the two respective probblems IF AND ONLY IF     pi(zi'x - bi) = 0 //foralli     ((c_j - p'Aj) xj = 0 //forallj
LinearProgramming.Which inequality signs do 'max' primal p: If the primal is max then the constraints of the dual will be inequalities similar to the variables of the primal.
LinearProgramming.Which inequality signs to 'min' primal p: If the primal is min then the variables of the dual will be inequalities similar to the opposite constriant.
LinearProgramming.primal optimality and dual feasibility?: Primal optimality condition is that c' - cB'B-1A \ge 0' is the same as the dual feasibility condition p'A \le c'
LinearProgramming.a primal algorithm?: Any method that maintains primal feasibility while working towards dual feasibility is called a primal algorithm.
LinearProgramming.a dual algorithm?: A dual algorithm maintains dual feasibility and works towards primal feasibility.
LinearProgramming.Is the simplex method a primal algorithm: The simplex method is an algorithm that maintains primal feasibility while working towards dual feasibility.
LinearProgramming.si the initial tableau of the dual simpl: Given a problem in standard form (with rows of A being linearly independent) let B be a basis matrix and c a cost vectr. Then the dual simplex forms the tableau     -cB'B-1b | //bar{c}'     B-1b | B-1A
LinearProgramming.the dual simplex method and the vector: The dual simplex method does not require B-1b to be nonnegative (so our solution may nor may not be feasible to the primal).
The dual simplex method requires that //bar{c} \ge 0, which is the same as saying that cB'B-1 \le c'      (this means that we have a feasible solution to the dual)
LinearProgramming.form of the problems for the dual simpl: The dual simplx method starts with a primal problem in standard form.
LinearProgramming.When is the dual simplex method complete: If the inequalit B-1b \ge 0 holds in the dual simpex method, then our solution has become primal feasible and we're done.
LinearProgramming.When does the dual simplex method requir: If the inequality B-1b \le 0 fails to hold, we must perform a change of basis.
LinearProgramming.change of basis procedure for the dual: The change of basis procedure in the dual simplex method is as follows:     1. find a row of the tableau with xB(l) \le 0 (this is the povot row)     2. Form the ration //bar{c}i / |v_i| and let j be the index for which this ration is smallest, where vi //lt 0      (the entry vj is called the pivot element. )     3. xj must be nonbasic since there is a negative number in its col.      4. Let column Aj enter the basis and column AB(l) exit.
LinearProgramming.signs of the pivot element and reduced c: If our primal is min, then the pivot element v_j in the Dual Simplex Method is always chosen to be negative, and the corresponding reduced cost //bar{c}_jis nonnegative.
LinearProgramming.the reduced cost of every nonbasic varia: If the reduced cost of every nonbasic variable is nonzero, then the dual cost will increase with each basis change, and no basis will ever be repeated in the course of the algorithm.

If the reduced cost of every nonbasic variable is zero, the algorithm must eventually terminate and this can happen in one of two ways:      (a) We have B^{-1}b \ge 0 and have and optimal solution.      (b) All of the entries v_1, ..., v_n in the pivot row are nonnegative and we are therefore unable to locate a pivot element. In full analogy with the primal simplex method, this implies that the optimal dual cost is equal to +//infty and the primal problem is infeasible.
LinearProgramming.good to turn to lexicographic ordering?: In the case that one dual variable has reduced cost of zero, the general pivot rules may cause a cycle. So in this case, its good to turn to lexicographic ordering.
LinearProgramming.Lexicographic Pivoting Rule?: Lexicographic Pivoting Rule for the Dual Simplex Method:      1. Choose any row [script l] such that x_B([script l]) //lt 0, to be hte pivot row.      2. Determine the index j of the entering column as follows. For each column with v_i //lt 0, divide all entries by |v_i|, and then choose the lexicographically smallest column. If there is a tie between several lexicographically smallest columns, choose the one with the smallest index.
LinearProgramming.dual simplex method be used?: The dual simplex method should be used when a basic feasible solution to the dual problem is readily avaliable.
LinearProgramming.solve for the dual solution of a problem: We can solve for the dual solution if we know the basis matrix: p^TA_{B(i)} = c_{B(i)}, i = 1, ..., m. THis leads to m equations and m unknowns, and since the columns of A are linearly independent there is a unique solution p.
LinearProgramming.a geometric iteration of the dual simple: A geometric iteration of the dual simplex method: At every iteration we have a basic feasible solution to the dual problem. THe basuc feasible solutions obtained at any two consecutive iterations have m - 1 linearly independent active constraints in common; thus consecutive basic feasible solutions are either adjacent or they coincide.
LinearProgramming.dual degeneracy relate to?: Dual degeneracy relates to having more than m reduced costs that are zero. (whenever there is a nonbasic variable whose reduced cost is zero).
LinearProgramming.basis elements relate to solutions for: Every basis determines a basic solution to the primal, but also a corresponding basic solution to the dual, namely p^T = c_B^TB^{-1}.
LinearProgramming.feasible dual basic solutions and reudc: A dual basic solution is feasible IF AND ONLY IF all of the reduced costs are nonnegative.
LinearProgramming.reduced costs of zero imply about a dual: Under a dual basic solution, the reduced costs that are equal to zero correspond to active constraints in the dual problem.
LinearProgramming.degenerate dual basic solutions and reu: A dual basic solution is degenerate IF AND ONLY IF some nonbasic variable has zero reduced cost.
LinearProgramming.Farakas' Lemma?: Farakas' Lemma: Let A be a matrix of dimensions m \times n nad let B be a vector in \mathbb{R}^n. Then exactly one of the following two alternatives holds:      (a) There exists some x \ge 0 such that Ax = b.      (b) There exists some vector p such that p^TA \ge 0^T and p^Tb //lt 0.
LinearProgramming.148: Let A_1, ..., A_n and b be given vectors and suppose that any vector p that satisfies p^TA \ge 0, i = 1, ..., n, must also satisfy p^Tb \ge 0. Then b can be expressed as a nonnegative linear combination of the vectors A_1, ..., A_n.
LinearProgramming.149: SUppose that the system of linear inequalities Ax \le b has at least one solution, and let d be some scalar. Then the following are equivalent:      (a) Every feasible solution to the system Ax \le b satisfies c^Tx \le d.      (b) There exists some p \ge 0 such that p^TA = c and p^Tb \le d.
LinearProgramming.150: Let S be a nonempty closed convex subset of \mathbb{R}^n and let x* be a vector that does not belong to S. Then there is some vector c \in \mathbb{R}_b such that c^Tx* //lt c^Tx for all x \in S.
LinearProgramming.151: A set C \subsetne \mathbb{R}^n is a cone if \lambdax \in C for all \lambda \ge 0 and all x \in C.
LinearProgramming.152: If the sero vector is an extreme point of a cone, then we say the the cone is pointed.
LinearProgramming.153: Let C \subsetne \mathbb{R}^n be the polyhedral cone defined by the constraints a_i^Tx \ge 0, i = 1, ...., m. Then THE FOLLOWING ARE EQUIVALENT: pbr]1. The zero vector is an extreme point of C.      2. THe cone C does not contain a line.      3. There exist n vectors out of the damuly a_1, ..., a_m that are linearly independent.
LinearProgramming.154: Let P be a nonempty polyhedron and let y \in P. We define the recession cone at y as the set of all directions d along which we can move indefinitely awy from y, without having to leave the set P. More formally, the recession cone is defined as the set {d \in \mathbb{R}^n | A(y + \lambdad) \ge b, for all \lambda \ge 0 } = {d \in \mathbb{R}^n | Ad \ge 0 }
LinearProgramming.155: The nonzero elements of the recession cone are called the rays of the polyhedton P.
LinearProgramming.156: A nonzero element x of a polyhedral cone C \subsetne \mathbb{R}^n is called an extreme ray if there are n - 1 linearly independent constraints that are active at c.
LinearProgramming.157: An extreme ray of the recession cone associated with a nonempty polyhedron P is called an extreme ray of P.
LinearProgramming.158: Two extreme rays are equivalent if one cone is a positive multiple of the other.
LinearProgramming.159: A finite collection of extreme rays will be said to be a complete set of extreme rays if it contains exactly one ray from each equivalence class.
LinearProgramming.160: Consider the problem of minimizing c^Tx over a pointed polyhedral cone C = x \in \mathbb{R}^n | a_i^Tx \ge 0, i = 1, ..., m }. The optimal cost is equal to -//infty IF AND ONLY IF some extreme ray d of C satisfies c^T //lt 0.
LinearProgramming.161: Consider the problem of minimizing c^Tx subject to Ax \ge b, and assume that the feasible set has at least one extreme point. The optimal cost is equal to -//infty IF AND ONLY IF some extreme ray d of the feasible set saitisfies c^T //lt 0.
LinearProgramming.162: Resolution Theorem:      Let P = { x \in \mathbb{R}^n | Ax \ge b } be a nonempty polyhedron with at least one extreme point. Let x^1, ..., x^k be extreme points and let w^1, ..., w^r be a complete set of extreme rays of P.      Let Q = \sigma_{i = 1 to k}\lambda_ix^i + \sigma_{j = 1 to r}\theta_jw^j | \lambda_i \ge 0, \theta_j \ge 0, \sigma_{i = 1 to k}\lambda_i = 1 }      Then Q = P.
LinearProgramming.163: A nonempty bounded polyhedron is the convexhull of its extreme points.
LinearProgramming.164: Assume that the cone C = { x | Ax \ge 0} is pointd. Then every element of C can be expressed as a nonnegative linear combination of the extreme raysof C.
LinearProgramming.165: A set Q is finitely generated if it is specified in the form      Q = { \sigma_{i = 1 to k}\lambda_ix^i + \sigma_{j = 1 to r}\theta_jw^j | \lambda_i \ge 0, \theta_j \ge 0, \sigma_{i = 1 to k}\lambda_i = 1.
LinearProgramming.166: A finitely generated set is a polyhedron. In particular, the convex hull of finitely many vectors is a (bounded) polyhedron.
